coredata.dc:description: Traditional fossil fuels significantly contribute to energy supply, economic development, and advancements in science and technology. However, prolonged and extensive use of fossil fuels has resulted in increasingly severe environmental pollution. Consequently, it is imperative to develop new, clean, and pollution-free energy sources with high energy density and versatility as substitutes for conventional fossil fuels, although this remains a considerable challenge. Simultaneously, addressing water pollution is a critical concern. The development, design, and optimization of functional nanomaterials are pivotal to advancing new energy solutions and pollutant remediation. Emerging porous framework materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs), recognized as exemplary crystalline porous materials, exhibit potential in energy and environmental applications due to their high specific surface area, adjustable pore sizes and structures, permanent porosity, and customizable functionalities. This work provides a comprehensive and systematic review of the applications of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in emerging energy technologies, including the oxygen reduction reaction, oxygen evolution reaction, hydrogen evolution reaction, lithium-ion batteries, and environmental pollution remediation such as the carbon dioxide reduction reaction and environmental pollution management. In addition, strategies for performance adjustment and the structure-effect relationships of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives for these applications are explored. Interaction mechanisms are summarized based on experimental discussions, theoretical calculations, and advanced spectroscopy analyses. The challenges, future prospects, and opportunities for tailoring these materials for energy and environmental applications are presented.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional fossil fuels significantly contribute to energy supply, economic development, and advancements in science and technology. However, prolonged and extensive use of fossil fuels has resulted in increasingly severe environmental pollution. Consequently, it is imperative to develop new, clean, and pollution-free energy sources with high energy density and versatility as substitutes for conventional fossil fuels, although this remains a considerable challenge. Simultaneously, addressing water pollution is a critical concern. The development, design, and optimization of functional nanomaterials are pivotal to advancing new energy solutions and pollutant remediation. Emerging porous framework materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs), recognized as exemplary crystalline porous materials, exhibit potential in energy and environmental applications due to their high specific surface area, adjustable pore sizes and structures, permanent porosity, and customizable functionalities. This work provides a comprehensive and systematic review of the applications of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in emerging energy technologies, including the oxygen reduction reaction, oxygen evolution reaction, hydrogen evolution reaction, lithium-ion batteries, and environmental pollution remediation such as the carbon dioxide reduction reaction and environmental pollution management. In addition, strategies for performance adjustment and the structure-effect relationships of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives for these applications are explored. Interaction mechanisms are summarized based on experimental discussions, theoretical calculations, and advanced spectroscopy analyses. The challenges, future prospects, and opportunities for tailoring these materials for energy and environmental applications are presented.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive use of fossil fuels, coupled with the rapid advancement of the economy and industry, has resulted in energy crises and environmental pollution, attracting global concern. 1 This has led to notable environmental issues and social challenges, such as air and water pollution, ecological degradation, climate change, and adverse effects on human health. The introduction of the “dual-carbon policy” further highlights the urgency for change in energy and environmental sectors. 2 As a result, there has been increasing focus on the development of clean energy sources (e.g., hydrogen energy, lithium-ion batteries [LIBs]) and technologies for controlling environmental pollution.

A wide range of traditional materials have been employed in clean energy technology and environmental pollution management (EPM), particularly noble metal-based materials (Pt, Pb, Au, Ir, Pt), known for their excellent dynamics and pH universality. 3 However, their high cost and limited stability under prolonged oxidation conditions constrain their use. 4 Recently, non-noble metal-based and metal-free alternatives, such as metal oxides, transition metals, and carbon-based catalysts, have gained attention. 5
,
6
,
7 Yet, issues such as slow reaction rates, low catalytic efficiency, and poor recyclability continue to limit their application. In contrast to traditional materials, emerging porous framework materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have been widespread application, especially in clean energy and EPM.

MOFs, a class of porous frameworks with infinite lattices, are formed by metal cations and polydentate organic ligands through coordination bonds. 8 MOFs are considered promising materials for energy and environmental applications due to their significant porosity, adjustable pore sizes, and uniformly distributed metal components. More than 20,000 MOFs with different structural forms such as one-dimension (1D), two-dimension (2D), three-dimension (3D), have been synthesized by different combinations of metals 8 and organic linkers since the discovery by Yaghi et al. 9 in the late 1990s for various specialized applications. The distinct advantages of MOFs enable exceptional performance in various applications, including gas storage and separation, 10
,
11 magnetic and molecular recognition, 12
,
13 LIBs, 14
,
15
,
16 catalysis, 17
,
18 EPM, 19
,
20
,
21 and drug delivery. 22 COFs, another kind of crystalline porous materials, consist of organic molecules that are linked by strong covalent bonds, 23 possessing ordered pore structures and periodical and tunable skeletons. COFs offer the distinct benefits of permanent porosity, high stability, low density, and adjustable skeletons and pore structures, which support their use in applications such as gas capture/separation, 24
,
25 radionuclide elimination, 26
,
27 proton conduction, 28
,
29 catalysis, 30
,
31 and energy storage. 32 Then their derivatives have been well studied because a porous network can act as a desired host for encapsulation of many other functional materials. 33
,
34 MOFs have attracted significant attention due to their large specific surface area and pore size. However, COFs demonstrate superior stability. Within MOFs, those containing high-valence metal ions, such as aluminum and zirconium, exhibit greater stability. Due to the limited cycling performance of MOFs, their derivatives have been extensively studied and have shown promising properties. To date, the applications of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives have garnered significant interest. For example, Xiao et al. 35 reviewed the development prospects and challenges of MOF-based materials in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR), nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR), oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Huang et al. focused on COFs based on phthalocyanine (Pc-) and porphyrin (Por-) and their electrocatalytic performance in CO2RR, ORR, and water splitting. 36 Liu et al. provided a comprehensive overview of porous framework materials, including COFs, HOFs (hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks), and MOFs, for energy and environmental applications. 37 Although various reviews have been published on CO2RR, electrocatalysts, water oxidation, and EPM, a systematic and comprehensive summary of porous framework materials and their derivatives in the context of energy and environmental fields is still lacking.

In this review, a detailed summary of the applications of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in energy and environmental sectors is provided. The synthesis of these materials is first summarized. Following this, the latest advancements in the use of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in CO2RR, ORR, OER, HER, LIBs, and EPM are discussed in depth, with examples of typical materials shown in Figure 1 . Interaction mechanisms are explored from both macroscopic and microscopic perspectives, including experimental performance, advanced characterization techniques, and theoretical calculations. The structure-activity relationships of different materials are also discussed and illustrated (Figure 2 ). Finally, the challenges and potential future directions for the use of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in energy and environmental applications are presented. The important role of these materials in the evolution of the energy and environmental fields is outlined in the summary and presented in Table 1 , with the aim of offering insights for future research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para:
The ideal synthesis methods enable a wide range of experiments to examine material performance, providing crucial insights for designing advanced materials for future applications. MOF derivatives exhibit improved stability and conductivity, offering greater potential for expanded uses. Thus, the development of effective synthesis methods for MOFs and their derivatives is essential for their applications. The most commonly used method for MOF synthesis involves self-assembly between inorganic metal ions and organic ligands using a one-pot approach, seen in materials such as MOF-5, 131
,
132 ZIF-8, 133 UiO-66, 134 MIL-120, 135 MOF-210, 131 and CAU-1. 134 This method is straightforward but can sometimes result in hybrid complexes. Modulated synthesis, which involves the use of modulators to produce single crystals or highly crystalline products, coordinates with metals or suppresses the deprotonation of linkers to adjust the coordination equilibrium. The key to achieving high crystallinity is to slow down the kinetics of crystal growth. Schaate et al. applied this method to synthesize Zr-MOFs, 136 exploring the impact of monocarboxylic acids on the formation of UiO-type MOFs. However, this approach requires a long adjustment period and high-quality crystals. Beyond the two primary methods, other approaches include the microemulsion process, 137 template strategy, 138
,
139 interfacial growth method, 140 and spray-drying technique. 141

Due to the limited stability and conductivity of pristine MOFs, various feasible methods have been developed to prepare their derivatives for optimizing MOF performance. MOF materials can be transformed into monatomic or diatomic materials, metallic oxides, metal nanoparticles or clusters, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), and others, depending on the specific application or ingredient requirements. 142 Controlled thermal treatments are typically employed to convert MOFs into derived materials, where organic linkers and metallic nodes are transformed into heteroatom-doped carbon substrates and nanoparticles embedded in the carbon matrix, respectively. 143 For instance, Ma and co-workers synthesized MOF-derived Ni/NPC (N-doped porous carbon) materials through thermal treatment in 2024. 144 Controlled post-processing facilitates the maximal utilization of active metals within the carbon matrix. 145
,
146 In addition, alternative methods for synthesizing MOF-derived materials include wet chemical, 147
,
148 hydrothermal, 149 chemical vapor deposition, 150 photochemical, 151 mechanical, 152 and electrochemical methods. 153 Post-treatment-derived materials demonstrate improved stability and conductivity, broadening their applicability in energy and environmental fields.

COFs, porous materials with customizable structures, are constructed through covalent bonds from organic molecular precursors. 154 The development of efficient synthesis methods for COFs enhances operability and reduces costs, enabling the production of derivatives. The flexibility of COF synthesis allows the composition and functionality to be adjusted by designing building units or modifying pristine COFs post-synthesis. The solvothermal method 155 is the most commonly employed synthesis approach and was recently utilized by Zhu et al. 156 During this process, reactive substances, catalysts, and solvents are thoroughly mixed, vacuum-sealed, frozen with liquid nitrogen, and heated under constant temperature conditions. The resulting solid is then removed, washed, and vacuum-dried to obtain COFs. The complexity and stringent requirements of this method make COF synthesis more expensive than MOFs. Common catalytic methods include acid-driven 157 and transition metal catalyst-driven approaches. 158 Additional methods for producing crystalline porous COFs include ionothermal synthesis, 159 microwave synthesis, 160
,
161 mechanical grinding, 162
,
163 interface synthesis, 164
,
165 sonochemical synthesis, 166 and continuous synthesis. Porphyrin-based COFs are particularly notable in energy catalysis due to their active center sites. 66 For EPM applications, the incorporation of functional groups such as imidazole, crown ether, amido, and carboxylate enhances target affinity.

The potential of COFs as precursors for the preparation of efficient electrocatalytic carbon materials is enhanced by their nature as crystalline porous polymers, providing greater applicability. Thermal treatment, the most commonly employed method, has been effectively utilized to prepare COF-derived carbon-based electrocatalysts. 73 High-temperature treatment facilitates carbonization, phosphorization, and the incorporation of metal ions, thereby optimizing electrocatalytic performance. 48
,
73 In addition, COF-derived electrocatalysts can be synthesized using methods such as doping, sonication, vacuum tube sealing, and other techniques, employing COFs as precursors and carbon nanotubes as substrates. 167 Efforts to enhance electrocatalytic performance through the regulation of structural defects, active sites, conductivity, and the integration of ultrafine metal nanoparticles remain an area of ongoing research for COF-derived carbon-based electrocatalysts.

The synthesis strategies for MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives are illustrated in Figure 3 . Simultaneously, understanding the reaction mechanisms and application properties of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives is crucial due to their tunable properties and vast potential for applications in diverse advanced technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Usage of porous nanomaterials and their derivatives in energy and environmental applications

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive use of global fossil fuels has led to significant environmental challenges, particularly due to high CO2 emissions. Electrocatalytic CO2RR offers a promising approach to mitigate CO2 concentrations by converting excess CO2 into valuable chemicals and fuels. However, the inherent stability of CO2 molecules necessitates the development of durable and efficient catalysts to enable the CO2RR process. Porous nanomaterials, with their advantageous structures and selectivity, demonstrate considerable potential in advancing CO2RR applications. 38

The CO2RR process primarily involves electron transfer, with the resultant products influenced by factors such as reaction conditions, catalyst composition, and the availability of electrons and protons. The main steps in CO2RR are as follows: (1) CO2 molecules are adsorbed onto the catalyst’s active sites, allowing electron and proton donation to form CO2∗ (∗denotes the surface-bond site), (2) additional electron and proton donation leads to the intermediate product COOH∗, (3) dehydration of COOH∗ generates CO∗, which reacts with the catalyst to produce CO, (4) CO∗ may further accept electrons and protons, resulting in the formation of products such as CH4, C2H4, and CH3OH. 168

The intrinsic limitations of original MOF structures, such as low conductivity and stability, constrain their application in CO2RR. The metal species and their coordination environments play a crucial role in determining the accessibility and reactivity of active sites, leading to significant attention on diverse metal centers in MOFs. 169

Cu-based MOFs: Cu-based MOFs are the most extensively used electrocatalysts for CO2RR due to their ability to produce diverse products, including hydrocarbons, aldehydes, and alcohols. For example, Kung et al. achieved a 28% FEformate in CO2RR by incorporating Cu into MOFs. 49 To enhance selectivity and stability, Liu et al. developed Cu-N structured MOFs (CuPz2-Act-30) as electrocatalysts, capable of selectively reducing CO2 to C2H2 at −1.03 V vs. RHE with an FE of 70.2% ± 1.7% (Figure 4 A). 50 Observations of adsorbed intermediates during CO2RR are illustrated in Figure 4B. Yan et al. demonstrated that tandem catalysis using a serial connection of Cu-based MOF and copper foil (Cu-MOF-CF) effectively suppressed CH4 production while increasing C2H4 selectivity. 51 In addition, the introduction of conductive materials such as Ketjen Black (KB) 52 or functional groups such as halogen atoms 44 further enhanced the performance of Cu-based MOFs in CO2RR.

Ni- and Co-based MOFs: Ni- and Co-based MOFs have also shown potential in CO2RR. Lin et al. fabricated layered Ni@C hollow spheres containing Ni nanoparticles (Ni NPs). 170 Yi et al. incorporated Ni-phthalocyanine molecules into Ni-based MOFs, synthesizing NiPc-NiO4 nanosheets. 53 Xin et al. enhanced MOF-545-Co with metallocene species, resulting in a strong binding affinity between the metallocene and metalloporphyrin moieties and achieving a FECO of 97% (Figure 4C). 40 Furthermore, Xin et al. synthesized PPy@MOF-545-Co, which attained an FECO of 98% at −0.8 V, demonstrating that PPy significantly improved electron transfer to active sites. 46

Bi-based MOFs: Recent research has highlighted the exceptional performance of Bi-based MOFs in CO2RR, particularly for the efficient production of formate. Zhang et al. developed Bi-N4 sites on porous carbon networks, achieving a FECO of 97% and a high turnover frequency (TOF) of 5,535 h−1 at 0.39 V. 54 Cao et al. synthesized ultrathin bismuthenes (Bi-enes) derived from Bi-MOF as cathode catalysts, achieving nearly 100% FEformate in the voltage range of 1.6–3.0 V. 55 Furthermore, Huang et al. designed a porous Bi-based MOF (Bi-BTC), which exhibited a FEformate of 96% at −0.9 V (vs. RHE). 56 These findings demonstrate that Bi-based MOFs significantly reduce the activation energy barriers for intermediates, thereby enhancing formate selectivity. Nam et al. further investigated pore size and Lewis acidity, revealing that smaller channel dimensions improve catalytic activity in CO2RR. 57

Metal coordination environments within COFs can be modulated using various methods, including the incorporation of porphyrins, phthalocyanines, and bipyridines. 171 Metalloporphyrins have attracted significant interest in CO2RR due to their high selectivity, activity, and stability. 58
,
59 Zhu et al. synthesized a series of M-TTCOFs using different metalloporphyrins. Among these, Co-TTCOF achieved a FECO of 91.3% at −0.7 V, with an onset potential of −0.45 V in KHCO3 solution (Figures 4D and 4E). 60
Figure 4F depicts the reduction of Co(II) to Co(I), which interacted with CO to form the intermediate Co(II)∗COOH, leading to CO generation and completion of CO2RR. Liu et al. developed a tripartite Co porphyrin COF (CoTAPP-PATA-TAPZ-COF) (Figure 4G), which achieved an impressive FE of 97.2% at −0.8 V. 61 Cheung et al. employed an environmentally friendly method to synthesize Fe(III) porphyrin COFs, demonstrating excellent TOF and FE in CO2RR. 172

Zhang et al. integrated metal phthalocyanines with M-N4 structural motifs as active sites into 2D COFs, achieving an impressive FE exceeding 93% across a voltage range of −0.6 to −1.1 V. 62 Guo et al. combined NiPc-COF with Salen(Co)2, which featured Co-N2O2 active sites, demonstrating high-efficiency CO2RR even under an O2 atmosphere. 63 Lu et al. synthesized metal phthalocyanine COFs (M = Zn, Ni, Co) using a network chemistry strategy. 64 Among them, NiPc-TFPN COF achieved an exceptional FECO of 99.8% at −0.9 V. Under illumination, the current density was further enhanced, and NiPc-TFPN COF maintained a nearly 100% FECO within a potential window of −0.8 to −0.9 V (Figures 4H and 4I). CoPc-PI-COF demonstrated remarkable thermal stability (exceeding 300°C) and chemical stability (resisting 12 M HCl for 20 days). 65 Techniques such as functional group modulation, 66 incorporation of functionalized carbon nanotubes, 67 and transition metal modification 173 show great promise for advancing CO2RR.

Crystalline porous frameworks synthesized via the coordination of organic ligands with metal centers provide enlarged surface areas, facilitating the accessibility of CO2 molecules to active sites. This architecture promotes efficient charge and proton transfer, enhancing CO2RR performance. The high CO2RR activity, selectivity, and current density are attributed to well-dispersed active sites, which optimize the adsorption and binding of reaction intermediates during the electrochemical process. However, differences in product selectivity across materials with varied substrates require further exploration. Investigating the synergistic effects of bimetallic or multimetallic components in catalytic systems is another promising direction. Although these materials exhibit notable catalytic efficiency, stability, and recyclability remain areas for improvement, necessitating continued research in this field.

ORR is a critical process in electrochemical energy conversion systems such as fuel cells. 174
,
175
,
176 While Pt-based electrocatalysts are highly efficient for ORR, their high cost and limited stability restrict their broader application. 43
,
177 To address these challenges, MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives have emerged as promising ORR catalysts. Their high porosity and large specific surface area enhance oxygen transfer and provide efficient charge transport pathways. 178
,
179 Derivatives of MOFs, such as carbonized MOFs with transition metals, exhibit improved conductivity and stability, making them particularly effective for ORR. Similarly, COFs, especially those combined with transition metals or structurally modified, demonstrate considerable potential as ORR catalysts.

The ORR involves a complex series of electrochemical reactions characterized by multi-step electron transfers and various oxygen-containing intermediates. Despite extensive research, the exact reaction mechanism remains unresolved. During ORR, molecular oxygen is electrochemically reduced via two primary pathways. The four-electron pathway yields H2O in acidic media or OH− in alkaline media, while the two-electron pathway produces H2O2 in acidic media or HO2 − in alkaline media as intermediate products. 180

The extensive pore channels of MOFs offer good accessibility to active sites. Pristine MOFs have been applied to ORR, 182 but their low conductivity and poor electrochemical stability limit their application. Recent studies have focused on MOF-derived carbon materials. Figure 5 A illustrates the synthesis of ultrathin N-doped carbon nanosheets (NCNSs) containing intrinsic defects through the pyrolysis of ZIF-8. 68 Zhong et al. synthesized hollow Co-anchored N-doped carbon material (A-CoNC). 69 As shown in Figure 5B, the low Tafel slope is 55.5 mV dec−1, with a significant limited current density of 5.37 mA cm−2. In addition, single-atom catalysts (SACs) with tunable active sites and high atomic utilization are often derived from MOFs as precursors. For instance, MOF-5 was used as a precursor to synthesize Fe SAC-MOF-5, 70 achieving an atomic Fe content of up to 2.35 wt % and ORR activity with an E1/2 of 0.83 V (vs. RHE) in 0.5 M H2SO4, outperforming both control catalysts and most platinum group metal-free catalysts (Figure 5C). Furthermore, bimetallic catalysts can induce a synergistic effect by introducing more active sites. A Co-Cu bimetallic MOF achieved onset and half-wave potential values of 1.06 and 0.95 V vs. RHE, respectively (Figure 5D). 71

In conclusion, incorporating functional groups such as porphyrin and phthalocyanine, as well as adding other active components, is an effective strategy for enhancing the electrocatalytic activity of MOFs. MOF derivatives obtained through appropriate methods exhibit excellent conductivity and stability while maintaining the porous structure of MOFs.

COFs have recently attracted significant attention as promising electrocatalysts for ORR due to their capability to precisely position active sites. 39
,
87
,
183 Lin et al. reported the synthesis of two COFs with expanded interlayer spacing. 72 Theoretical calculations revealed that the optimized DPPS-COF exhibited significantly enhanced ORR activity (Figure 5E). Furthermore, COFs can be integrated with various transition metal ions to increase the density of active sites and enhance conductivity for ORR. The CoOx@NC-800 electrocatalyst achieved an E1/2 of 0.89 V and demonstrated high long-term stability, retaining 100% of its current after 3,000 CV cycles. 73
Figure 5F illustrates the atomic structures of the models and intermediates involved in the reactions. SACs are recognized as potential substitutes for Pt/C in ORR due to their superior atom utilization and exceptional chemical stability. Yang et al. developed 2D carbons derived from a fully conjugated COF (Fe-COF900), which immobilized paired Fe atoms on the surface. 74 This material exhibited an E1/2 of 0.82 V and a mass activity of 4,087.9 mA mg−1 at 0.7 V vs. RHE, outperforming Pt/C (Figure 5G).

Overall, the catalytic activity and conductivity of COFs can be improved through the introduction of conjugated bridges (such as olefins and oligothiophenes) and heteroatom doping (e.g., nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus). Metal-based MOFs incorporating transition metals exhibit excellent ORR performance. SACs are highly valued for their superior activity and stability. Moreover, COFs containing metal porphyrin or phthalocyanine units exhibit outstanding ORR performance. Despite these advancements, challenges remain in enhancing the efficiency of MOF/COF-based electrocatalysts for ORR. Key considerations include: (1) designing MOF/COF-based materials with high stability and conductivity by leveraging DFT calculations to maximize their potential applications and (2) addressing the immaturity of structural simulations and theoretical calculations for MOF/COF-based materials. Further exploration of mechanisms through in situ monitoring and characterization techniques (e.g., in situ XPS, Raman spectroscopy, etc.) is required.

The anodic OER is a critical half-reaction in water electrolysis, but its complex four-electron transfer mechanism leads to challenges such as sluggish kinetics, high overpotential, and low energy efficiency. 184
,
185 Consequently, efficient and stable OER electrocatalysts are essential for water electrolysis. Due to their structural and chemical advantages, MOF/COF-based materials are frequently employed as electrocatalysts for OER, as they reduce reaction potential barriers and increase reaction rates. 75
,
186

OER represents a vital half-reaction in the water electrolysis process. However, it is more complex than the HER process due to the involvement of a four-electron transfer mechanism. To achieve effective reaction outcomes, OER requires a higher overpotential compared with HER. Based on theoretical models, OER proceeds through four distinct stages under both acidic and alkaline conditions, as outlined below: 181

The symbol ∗ denotes a surface adsorption site, while OH∗, O∗, and OOH∗ represent adsorbed intermediates. OER occurs more favorably in alkaline media compared with acidic conditions due to the slow kinetics of the initial step in acidic media, which involves water molecule decomposition. 77 Consequently, there is an urgent need for highly active electrocatalysts for OER.

MOFs can be directly employed as OER electrocatalysts, utilizing the benefits of both heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts. However, the electrochemical activity of MOFs is often limited by the insulating nature of organic ligands and the small overlap between the p orbitals of ligands and the d orbitals of metal ions. 187 To address these limitations, transforming bulk MOFs into ultrathin layers with nanometer-scale thickness of nanometers has emerged as an effective approach. Tian’s group developed a rapid (20 s) transformation method to produce ultrathin metal oxyhydroxide nanosheets for efficient OER (Figure 6 A). 75 Sun et al. visualized MTV-MOFs (multivariate MOFs) at the atomic scale using low-dose iDPC-STEM, revealing localized lattice strain upon the incorporation of Fe3+ or 2-aminoterephthalate into Ni-BDC, enhancing electrocatalytic activity. This material achieved overpotentials of 365 mV at 100 mA cm−2 and 286 mV at 10 mA cm−2 (Figure 6B). 47 Most MOFs, existing as powders, require polymer binders such as Nafion or polytetrafluoroethylene, which can obstruct active sites and impede mass transfer at the catalyst-electrolyte interface. 188 Fe-based MOF nanoneedle arrays on nickel foam, treated with UV/O3, demonstrated enhanced performance with a low Tafel slope of 38.8 mV dec−1 and an overpotential of 218 mV at 10 mA cm−2 (Figure 6C). 76 Furthermore, heterostructure catalysts, such as cMOF/LDH heterostructures on LDH nanowires, have shown improved electrocatalytic activity, achieving low overpotentials of 216 and 227 mV and current densities of 50 and 100 mA cm−2, along with a Tafel slope of 34.1 mV dec−1 (Figure 6D). 77

In summary, the limited electrical conductivity of MOFs and COFs poses a significant challenge for their practical application. A viable approach to improving the conductivity of MOFs involves preparing ultrathin MOF layers, incorporating conjugated organic linkers through coordination chemistry, or introducing conductive carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, carbon nanosheets, and graphene during synthesis. These derivatives maintain the porous architecture of the original MOFs while offering enhanced conductivity and stability. For example, Fe-based MOF derivatives obtained through carbonization exhibit exceptional OER performance due to the formation of highly active Fe-N-C sites. Similarly, derivatives containing transition metals such as Co and Ni, subjected to appropriate thermal treatments, demonstrate superior catalytic activity and durability.

Non-metallic COFs are less commonly studied for electrocatalytic OER applications due to their lack of essential active metal sites and inefficient free electron transport. However, it has been observed that introducing contact forces between conductive particles and the active material can improve electron transfer efficiency by preventing agglomeration. Wang et al. synthesized a pyrolysis-free ionic COF nanosheet without metal (JUC-627-NS), achieving an overpotential of 275 mV at 10 mA cm−2 via cation-π interactions. 78 Theoretical calculations and in situ FT-IR spectroscopy demonstrated that these interactions enhance OER performance (Figures 6E and 6F). In addition, Fe-SAC@COF with a unique Fe-NO coordination configuration exhibited remarkable OER activity, characterized by a low overpotential and Tafel slope. 79
Figure 6G illustrates the four-electron reaction pathways for the Fe-SAC cluster under alkaline conditions. 80

In general, while COFs provide stable and customizable frameworks, their limitations in OER performance stem from the absence of metal active sites and poor electron transport. However, incorporating conjugated bridges, such as olefins, oligothiophenes, and metal thiolates, can strengthen electronic interactions between organic linkers, thereby enhancing conductivity and catalytic efficiency. The application of metal-based COFs in OER is broader. Introducing monomers containing metal porphyrins or phthalocyanines and optimizing the type and electronic properties of the central metal ions can yield highly efficient COF-based electrocatalysts. Nevertheless, several critical issues remain in this domain: (1) developing MOF/COF-based electrocatalysts with high efficiency and resistance to acidic and alkaline environments for OER remains urgent and (2) the role of intermediates as active species in influencing OER catalytic activity has not been fully clarified. Advanced in situ characterization techniques and theoretical calculations are required to explore the primary intermediates involved in OER.

Hydrogen energy, as a pollution-free and energy-dense secondary energy source, is considered a crucial solution to address global climate change and ensure energy security. 189
,
190 Hydrogen production through electrocatalytic or photocatalytic water splitting is among the most optimal approaches, offering rapid, efficient, highly selective, and cost-effective processes. 191
,
192 Identifying highly active catalysts to improve HER efficiency is essential for achieving effective hydrogen production. 193
,
194 MOFs and COFs have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional catalytic activity, durability, and stability across a wide pH range. 195
,
42

The mechanism of HER varies between acidic and alkaline solutions, although both follow either the Volmer-Tafel or Volmer-Heyrovsky pathway. 196 In acidic solutions, the initial step is the Volmer reaction, where hydrogen protons adsorb onto the catalyst surface, and electrons from the catalyst surface combine with the catalytic active site to form hydrogen atoms. The second step proceeds via two pathways: in the presence of abundant H+ ions, H+ combines with Hads and electrons on the catalyst surface to produce hydrogen gas. This pathway is known as the Heyrovsky reaction. Alternatively, when Hads accumulates on the catalyst surface, Hads atoms directly combine to form H2 in a process termed the Tafel reaction. In alkaline or neutral electrolytes, HER follows a similar reaction pathway. However, due to the low H+ concentration in these environments, water molecules must first dissociate to produce H+ and OH− before the Volmer reaction can occur. The HER steps are outlined as follows. 181

where ∗ denotes the active site on the catalyst surface, and ads represents the adsorption state of intermediates, specifically Hads.

Photocatalytic HER involves three main steps. 197 The first step is the generation of electron-hole pairs by incident photons. The second step is the separation and diffusion of these charges to the catalyst surface without recombination of the photogenerated carriers. The final step is the reduction of surface-adsorbed species by the photogenerated electrons and holes, leading to the production of H2.

The evolution rate of H2 is directly proportional to the concentration of electrons, making electron concentration a critical factor in determining HER efficiency. MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives facilitate H2 generation by enhancing electron transport and optimizing the exposure of catalytic active sites. 198
,
199

Achieving high electrocatalytic HER efficiency relies on identifying a suitable electrocatalyst to minimize the reaction overpotential. 200
,
201 Cai et al. designed a flexible and scalable HOF template for synthesizing MOF nanotubes with various components (NiRu-MOF, NiIr-MOF, and NiRuIr-MOF) by reacting with different metal salts (Figure 7 A). 41 Specifically, the overpotential (13 mV) and Tafel slope (17.3 mV dec−1) of NiRuIr@NGTs were lower than those of previously reported HER electrocatalysts (Figure 7B). Qi et al. demonstrated efficient HER by incorporating plasma-induced active species into MOFs. 81 The resulting P-Ni MOF/POM achieved an overpotential of 35 mV@10 mA cm−2. Moreover, MOFs can combine photosensitive and catalytic components within a single material for photocatalytic HER. 202
,
203 Lan et al. integrated HER catalysts into photosensitive MOFs for visible-light-driven HER. 82
Figure 7C illustrates the crystal structure and photocatalytic HER mechanism of Ru-TBP. 204 Heterostructures can reduce the diffusion distances for ions and electrons during the electrocatalytic process, enhancing electrocatalytic activity due to their distinct components. The MOF-derived hollow CoSx@MoS2 heterostructure exhibited superior catalytic performance for HER, with a low onset overpotential (Figure 7D). Figure 7E displays the polarization curves of different products. The material showed an overpotential of 239 mV@10 mA cm−2 for HER in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 347 mV@10 mA cm−2 for OER in 1 M KOH. 83 A MOF-derived N-doped carbon-encapsulated Fe-Co-O/Co hybrid heterostructure showed 112 mV@10 mA cm−2 for HER. 84

COFs demonstrate notable stability, large specific surface area, tunable functionality, and low density. 205 Zhang and co-workers synthesized the PEDOT@Metal-Salen (PEDOT, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)) COFEDA and Metal-Salen COFEDA, achieving a Tafel slope of 43 mV dec−1 and an overpotential of 150 mV. 45 The pore size of PEDOT@Metal-Salen COFEDA was determined to be 1.7 nm (Figure 7F), confirming the successful incorporation of the PEDOT conductive 1D linear polymer into 1D channel arrays. Post-protonation to improve the hydrophilic properties and charge separation efficiency of COFs is widely applied in photocatalysis. 86
,
206 Gao et al. modified the exciton effect of COFs by introducing nitrogen and reducing the linker length atoms. 85 TP-BPN COF exhibited the lowest hydrogen production (only 214 μmol g−1 in 6 h) (Figure 7G). The HER efficiency of TP-BPyN PCOF was 12,276 μmol g−1 h−1. Dai et al. developed 2D π-conjugated bipyridine-based COFs for visible-light-driven HER. 86 The hybridization of COFs and MOFs can address their inherent drawbacks. 207
,
208 Hybridization of COF (CTF-1) with conductive MOF (Ni-CAT-1), exhibiting 8.03 mmol g−1 h−1, significantly reduces the charge transfer potential barrier and enhances the separation of photogenerated carriers (Figure 7H). 88

The regular channels and exceptionally high specific surface areas of MOFs and COFs create an ideal pathway for rapid ion conduction. These materials can leverage various structural features to offer multiple degrees of freedom, making them suitable as precursors/templates for the synthesis of derivatives. However, MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in HER must still address the following challenges: (1) the porosity and surface area are compromised during the production of their derivatives and (2) the development of highly efficient, long-lasting, and economically viable bifunctional HER catalysts is necessary to streamline device fabrication and reduce costs.

LIBs, as advanced energy storage systems, are composed primarily of positive and negative electrodes, electrolytes, and diaphragms. They are known for their high specific energy, long lifespan, high cycle stability, no memory effect, and safety, making them widely used in portable electronics, electric vehicles, and other applications. 209
,
210 However, LIBs continue to face challenges such as low energy density and poor charge/discharge performance. Therefore, discovering and designing materials with superior electrochemical performance is essential. This section reviews and summarizes the applications and mechanisms of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in LIBs.

The mechanism of lithium storage in LIBs involves the insertion and extraction of Li(I). During discharge, Li(I) moves from the positive electrode to the negative electrode, completing the insertion. During charging, Li(I) embedded on the surface of the negative electrode moves back to the positive electrode, completing the extraction. MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives primarily transfer Li(I) through insertion and extraction. The lithium storage in MOFs and COFs as electrode materials is attributed to the changes in the charge of metal ions or functional groups. Organic lithium storage groups, such as benzene rings, carbonyl groups, and carboxyl groups within MOFs and COFs, increase the number of active sites through electrochemical reactions, which in turn enhances the transport of Li(I). Guo et al. synthesized a multilayer hollow core material, CuO@NiO, which shows excellent electrochemical stability as an anode for LIBs. 95 As shown in Figure 8 A, the mesoporous structure formed between NiO and CuO NPs, as well as within the internal space of the microspheres, contributed to the excellent lithium storage performance of CuO@NiO. The unique multilayer hollow structure of CuO@NiO microspheres offered a large specific surface area for electrochemical reactions and added more active sites for Li(I) storage, which facilitated the diffusion and transport of Li(I). The insertion of Li(I) during the discharge process of CuO@NiO occurs in two stages. As depicted in Figure 8B, the initial discharge profile shows two stable discharge plateaus, which are assigned to the lithium insertion into CuO and NiO, respectively.

The conduction behavior of Li(I) in the solid-state composite polymer electrolyte is depicted in Figure 8C. 102 One portion of Li(I) migrates along the polyethylene oxide (PEO) molecular chain in a segmented manner, while the other part moves more rapidly through the COFs with their distinctive pores. Positively charged COFs interact electrostatically with TFSI–, promoting the movement of free Li(I) between the positive and negative electrodes, thereby facilitating the completion of the charging and discharging processes.

The unique porous structure of MOFs enhances the rapid migration and diffusion of Li(I). MOF-177 was the first to be used as an anode material in LIBs. 89 Following this, Zn-MOFs, Co-MOFs, Ni-MOFs, and Cu-MOFs were explored as anode materials. The development of MOF derivatives has significantly addressed the issue of poor electrochemical performance associated with MOFs. Common metal oxides derived from MOFs include monometallic oxides such as CuO, Co3O4, Fe2O3, and bimetallic oxides such as NiCo2O4
90 and ZnFe2O4. 91 Among these, porous Co3O4 tetrahedra 92 and porous Co3O4 parallel hexahedra 93 are used as a negative electrode in batteries, as they offer a large lithium storage capacity, excellent multiplier performance, and exceptional cycling stability.

MOF-derived porous carbon and nanocomposites are frequently employed as anode materials in LIBs. MOF-derived porous carbon materials combine the electrical conductivity of carbon with the porous structure of MOFs. 94 Nanocomposites, leveraging the unique porous structure of MOFs, combine with other highly conductive materials to effectively mitigate the volume expansion issue during repeated Li(I) insertion and extraction, while also enhancing the cyclic reversible capacity. 95
,
96 Wang et al. developed porous Cu2S@C as an anode material for Mg2SO4/Li2O4 hybrid batteries. 97 The specific capacity remained about 150 mA h g−1 after 50 cycles.

Liquid electrolytes, which are a crucial component of conventional LIBs, often contain trace impurities such as H2O/HF, which can negatively impact the stable cycling performance of LIBs. Sheng et al. utilized the MOF MIL-101(Cr) to construct a MOF-based LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2|Li battery, as shown in Figure 8D, achieving stability for more than 300 cycles even with 800 ppm H2O or 1,107 ppm acidic impurities. 98 The MIL-101(Cr) structure includes two open metal sites and independent F ions (Figure 8E).

COFs and their derivatives are widely used as both electrolytes and electrode materials in LIBs. 211 The lamellar stacking gap in 2D COFs provides a space for the de-embedding of Li(I). 212 Zhou et al. synthesized a piperazine-based 2D COFs anode material, which exhibited a high specific capacity of 1,644.3 mA h g−1 at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. 99 Chen et al. developed an N-doped Co3O4 carbon nanoelectrode film (H-Co3O4@CNBF), incorporating hollow Co3O4 nanomaterials, which retained a high reversible specific capacity of 540.0 mA g−1 h−1 after 200 cycles at a current density of 2 A g−1. 100 Moreover, introducing functional groups into COF structures as electrode materials proves to be highly efficient. For instance, PI-COF with C=N and C=O 101 and Janus dione-based COFs show an abundance of redox sites as cathodes, offering excellent electrochemical performance. 213

Solid-state electrolytes in LIBs exhibit high safety and extended lifetimes compared with conventional liquid electrolytes, and several COF-based solid-state electrolytes have been reported. Chen et al. addressed the issue of low ionic conductivity by incorporating PEO into the pore walls of COFs after the reverse incorporation of a cationic COF additive (EB-COF-TFSI) into the PEO matrix. 102 The optimized solid-state electrolyte containing cationic COFs demonstrated excellent reversible capacity and an ultra-long cycle life. Yang et al. synthesized a functionalized electrolyte (VCOF-SPE) as the primary component of the LiFePO4 | Li button battery. 103 In addition, innovative materials such as a single-ion conducting nitrogen hybrid-conjugated skeleton 214 and the COF-C16/PE membrane 104 have shown significant potential as COF-based solid-state electrolytes.

For LIBs, enhanced performance primarily arises from the functional ligands and open metal sites in MOFs, as well as redox organic building blocks in COFs. Redox-active metal clusters or organic ligands in MOFs create lithium storage sites, while functionalized COFs, characterized by high porosity and notable redox activity, offer ordered spatial volumes for lithium storage and active sites (e.g., C=N, -NH groups) that facilitate rapid Li+ transport. However, the complex synthesis process can sometimes lead to the formation of side reactions. The variability of metal species and organic units presents opportunities for developing multifunctional electrocatalysts. COFs have demonstrated superior stability compared with MOFs. Ongoing studies focus on enhancing performance through approaches such as the introduction of specific functional groups and composite materials. Consequently, the design and development of functionalized MOF/COF-derived composites with simplified synthesis and cost-effectiveness hold significant promise for the commercialization of LIBs.

The rapid development of human society has resulted in the emission of numerous pollutants into the environment, presenting challenges to the sustainable progress of society. 215
,
216 Environmental pollutants are typically categorized into two primary types: inorganic and organic pollutants. 217 Inorganic pollutants mainly consist of harmful heavy metal ions released by industrial activities. 218 Organic pollutants encompass dyes, antibiotics, and other waste generated during industrial processes. A variety of methods must be employed to manage or eliminate environmental pollutants to ensure environmental quality and public health. 219 Due to their unique physicochemical characteristics, MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives are widely utilized in EPM. 178
,
220
,
221
,
222

Heavy metal ions, a form of inorganic pollutant, present a significant environmental risk. 223 These ions are the ionic variants of metals or metal-like elements with relatively high atomic mass and density (typically more than five times that of water), including Cr(VI), Hg(II), As(V), Pb(II), and Cd(II), among others. 224
,
225 Various technologies are employed to reduce the concentration of heavy metal ions in wastewater, such as ion exchange, electrochemistry, adsorption, membrane filtration, and chemical precipitation. 226 Recently, MOFs have attracted considerable interest for heavy metal ion removal, such as luminescent Ln-MOF (NIIC-3-Ln) and EDTA-functionalized MOF (MOF-808-EDTA). 105
,
106 The pore size and surface area of MOFs are essential in heavy metal remediation. Li et al. introduced a mesoporous cationic Th-based MOF, SCU-8, featuring large channels with a 2.2 nm inner diameter and a high surface area of 1,360 m2 g−1, showing excellent anion exchange capacity for ReO4 − and Cr2O7 2−. 107 The removal efficiency of heavy metal ions can be significantly improved by incorporating specific functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, 108 isocyanate, thiourea, and isothiocyanate 109 ) into MOFs, creating MOFs/functional composites 110 or fabricating MOF-based membranes. 111

Organic pollutants are widespread in industrial and agricultural production. 227 These pollutants, primarily pharmaceuticals, are not easily degradable and can be transferred through the food chain, leading to disruptions in biodiversity. 221
,
228 Lin et al. demonstrated that Zr(IV)-based MOFs (MOF-808 and UiO-66), with outstanding adsorption capacities, effectively removed non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs from water (Figure 9 A). 112 The use of MOFs in combination with other materials to create heterojunctions plays an important role in the removal of pharmaceuticals. 113
,
114 Functionalizing MOFs with appropriate groups can significantly improve their efficiency in removing pharmaceuticals. 115

The adsorption mechanisms of MOFs for organic and inorganic pollutants are quite different. 219 For heavy metal ions, adsorption mainly involves coordination adsorption, ion exchange, and electrostatic interactions. 223 Ji et al. employed XPS, extended X-ray absorption fine structure, and DFT simulations, showing that the -COOH groups in MIL-121 specifically interact with heavy metals through inner-sphere coordination. 108 Wang et al. used FTIR, XPS, and zeta potential analysis to characterize the adsorption mechanism, finding that Pb(II) adsorption on Zr-MOF (Ni0.6Fe2.4O4-UiO-66-PEI) was related to chelation with imine/amine groups, while Cr(VI) adsorption involved electrostatic attraction, chelation, and redox reactions (Figure 9B). 116

MOFs primarily adsorb organic pollutants through physical adsorption, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and selective pore size exclusion. Their catalytic degradation involves activating reactants through redox reactions, photocatalysis, or enhanced reactions at active sites. 221
,
228 Zhao et al. reported that the removal of pharmaceuticals (tetracycline and diclofenac sodium) by magnetically functionalized Zr-MOF (Fe3O4@MOF-525) was mainly due to the increased micropore volume and surface area from defects, as well as the important roles of electrostatic interactions, π-π interactions, anion-π interactions, and hydrogen bonding. 117 Tang et al. found that the improved removal of rhodamine B and carbamazepine by BiOBr/MIL-53(Fe) MOFs was due to the heterojunction between MIL-53(Fe) and BiOBr, which hindered electron-hole pair recombination and enhanced visible light utilization, with -OH and H+ radicals being crucial for degradation (Figure 9C). 118

COFs are known for their superior chemical stability, thermal stability, and mechanical strength, which make them especially beneficial for EPM. 229
,
230
,
231 The distinctive physicochemical properties of COFs facilitate the efficient capture and removal of heavy metal ions, 232
,
233 such as COFs with hydrogen-bonded nanotraps (COF-HNU25) and fluorescent COFs (COF-LZU8). 119
,
120 Huang et al. reported a stable COF capable of effectively removing Hg(II) from water. 121 These structural attributes collectively enhance the capacity, efficiency, effectiveness, and versatility of COFs. Xiao et al. synthesized COF@PDA (PDA, polydopamine), achieving an adsorption capacity of 109.2 mg g−1 for Cu(II). 122 Jiang et al. created TpPa-NH2@EDTA by modifying COFs with EDTA, demonstrating excellent adsorption performance for metal ions, including soft Lewis acids (Ag(I), Pd(II)), borderline Lewis acids (Cu(II), Ni(II)), and hard Lewis acids (Fe(III), Cr(III)), with removal efficiencies greater than 85% within 5 min (Figure 9D). 123

The tunable pore sizes and chemical functionalization of COFs enable them to efficiently remove organic pollutants from water through adsorption mechanisms. 234 Zhang et al. developed a COF with chitosan as a film substrate, which effectively degraded 99.8% of paracetamol and 94.0% of bisphenol A in 180 min under visible light irradiation. 124 The polypyrrole-functionalized GO/COF-300 achieved high adsorption capacities of 115 mg g−1 for indomethacin and 138 mg g−1 for diclofenac from aqueous solutions under natural pH conditions. 125 Hao et al. synthesized a sulfonic acid-functionalized COF (COF-SO3H), which efficiently removed personal care products and pharmaceuticals from water and wastewater. 126

The removal mechanisms of pollutants by COFs mainly involve targeted molecular interactions, such as electrostatic attractions and specific functional group interactions within the COF’s porous framework. 232
,
235 COFs adsorb metal ions through coordination with functional groups and electrostatic interactions. 235 Wang et al. employed zeta potential and XPS analysis to demonstrate that the COF (Ni0.6Fe2.4O4-HT-COF) removed Pb(II) via electrostatic attraction and chelation (Figure 9E). 127 Merí-Bofí et al. found that triphenylbenzene (TPB)-dimethoxyterephthaldehyde (DMTP)-COF-SH exhibited superior adsorption of Hg(II) compared with leading thiol and thioether adsorbents. 128

COFs can efficiently remove organic pollutants through chemical interactions such as hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and hydrophobic interactions with functional groups such as hydroxyl, amine, or aromatic systems. 178 Zhuang et al. synthesized TPB-DMTP-COF, which effectively removed sulfamerazine from water. 129 Shi et al. highlighted the enhanced performance of COF TzDa/Ag/AgBr, attributing it to increased visible light absorption, a larger surface area, improved charge transfer and separation aided by Ag nanoparticles, and the prevention of Ag/AgBr aggregation via the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism (Figure 9F). 130 COFs are gaining attention for their potential in environmental remediation due to their highly ordered porous structures and chemical stability. They are expected to become more cost-efficient and adaptable for large-scale environmental applications.

In conclusion, this review has highlighted the recent progress of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in the energy and environmental sectors, including applications such as CO2RR, ORR, OER, HER, LIBs, and EPM. MOFs and COFs, as emerging classes of porous materials, exhibit distinct properties such as high surface area, tunable porosity, and chemical versatility, which make them ideal for energy and environmental applications. A detailed discussion has been provided on various strategies for modifying MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives, including functionalization, carbonization, and composite construction. In addition, we reviewed the reaction mechanisms for CO2RR, ORR, OER, HER, LIBs, and EPM, and explored the structure-performance relationships of MOFs, COFs, and their derivatives in these fields.

Although MOFs and COFs have achieved significant progress in energy and environmental applications, research is still in its early stages, and several challenges remain to be addressed in the future: (1) to meet industrial demands, scalable and green synthesis methods for large-scale production must be developed. (2) Mechanistic studies should combine theoretical calculations with advanced characterization techniques (including in situ methods) to better understand the fundamental processes in energy and environmental systems and detect intermediates during reactions. (3) Materials tailored for specific applications should be designed based on fundamental reaction principles, pre-screened using machine learning techniques, and experimentally validated. (4) When synthesizing derivatives, it is essential to preserve the material structure to ensure crystallinity and maintain performance. (5) Precise control of surface area and pore size for derivatives remains a challenge due to structural instability. (6) The development of COFs and MOFs with inherent conductivity has not yet been achieved.
